FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, 9-12 year olds recruited from 21 sites across the U.S., ABCD 4.0 release (2016-2019).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using multivariable linear regression of ABCD data examining prospective and cross-sectional associations between screen time modalities and MIND diet score.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Academic Pediatrics; no indication of duplication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 2 reports separate regression coefficients for texting (B=-0.15, 95% CI -0.24, -0.05, p=.01) and social media (B=-0.16, 95% CI -0.28, -0.05, p=.01) at Year 1 as IVs, with MIND diet nutrition score (a health/dietary outcome = individual-trait) as DV. Phone/SM-specific variables paired with an individual-trait outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric B coefficients with 95% CIs reported for 16 regression models in Table 2 and Table 3.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential regression analyses performed with quantitative results throughout.

FT_EC7_reason: SM/phone variables appear as IVs in multiple analyses (texting and social media predicting MIND diet score); EC7 does not apply.
notes: Both prospective and cross-sectional analyses present. Social media and texting as IVs with nutrition (MIND diet score) as DV in cross-sectional models.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes cross-sectional data from Year 3 of the ABCD Study (5.0 release), N=9859, 21 U.S. recruitment sites, ages 12-13 years.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using multiple linear regression of ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Annals of Epidemiology; no indication of duplication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Tables 2 and 3 report regression coefficients where texting time (hours/day, B=0.81, 95% CI 0.04-1.59, p=.040), social media time (B=0.83, 95% CI 0.06-1.61, p=.035), SMAQ score (B=0.43, 95% CI 0.17-0.69, p=.001), and MPIQ score (B=0.34, 95% CI 0.07-0.61, p=.012) are DVs with transgender/gender-questioning identity as the IV. Per v7 criteria, transgender/gender-questioning identity is explicitly an individual-trait (identity variable), qualifying as the individual-trait on the other side.

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric B coefficients with 95% CIs reported across 22 regression models in Tables 2 and 3.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analyses performed with quantitative results throughout.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional only, not longitudinal; EC7 requires a longitudinal design.
notes: SM/phone variables (texting time, social media time, SMAQ, MPIQ) are all DVs; transgender/gender-questioning identity is the IV. Gender identity qualifies as individual-trait per v7 explicit classification.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes prospective cohort data from Years 2-3 of the ABCD Study, N=9,398, 21 U.S. sites, ages 11-12 years.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis using logistic and linear regression of ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescent Health; no indication of duplication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 3 reports coefficients for 'Talk on the phone or text' (B=-0.17, 95% CI -0.22, -0.12, p<.001 for weekly sleep duration; OR=1.13, 95% CI 1.05-1.23, p=.002 for sleep disturbance) and 'Spend time online on social media' (B=-0.21, 95% CI -0.26, -0.16, p<.001; OR=1.10, 95% CI 1.01-1.20, p=.025) as IVs, with sleep outcomes (individual-trait health outcomes) as DVs.

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric OR and B coefficients with 95% CIs reported in Table 3 for all bedtime screen behaviors and sleep outcomes.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential regression analyses performed with quantitative results throughout.

FT_EC7_reason: SM/phone variables (texting, social media) appear as IVs predicting sleep outcomes; EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis, which is not the case here.
notes: Prospective cohort design (Year 2 exposure, Year 3 outcome). Phone/texting and social media are IVs; sleep (duration, disturbance) is the DV. All three papers INCLUDE.
